1.description: Upstream: Who provides warehouse lines? This figure presents the amount of warehouse lines of credit extended by each type of lenders to shadow banks. Mortgage banks are commercial banks that originate mortgages and have a record in the HMDA. Nonmortgage banks are commercial banks that do not have a record in the HMDA. Others include the GSE, real estate investment trusts (REITs), and other financial institutions. I calculate mortgage origination market shares using HMDA data. Sources: Warehouse lending market shares are calculated using shadow bank call report filings to state regulators.

2.description: Downstream: Nature of competition This figure compares the distribution of loan product type (panel A), borrower age (panel B), and borrower FICO (panels C and D) of loans originated by shadow banks and the banks that lend to shadow banks. Sources: Panels A and B use HMDA data from 2018 to 2019 because HMDA started collecting borrower age information since 2018. Panel C uses the Single-Family Loan Performance data disclosed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac from 2011 to 2017 to match with the main analysis sample period. Panel D uses the Loan Level Disclosure Data from Ginnie Mae from 2013 to 2017.

3.description: Funding relationship and downstream origination where I(fundingij=0) equals one if there is no funding relationship between bank i and shadow bank j and I(overlapij>0) has been defined above. Ratio′i captures share of shadow banks without a funding relationship with bank i but borrowing from bank i. Ratio′i serves as a benchmark for Ratioi⁠. For example, if bank i’s funding is all provided to shadow banks with nonzero mortgage market overlap with itself (i.e. Ratioi=1⁠), it could be driven by bank i’s originating mortgages in all counties, in which case Ratio′i would also be one. Yet, if Ratio′i is less than one, while Ratioi is equal to one, it suggests that bank i is more likely to lend to shadow banks with nonzero mortgage market overlap. Panel B makes a similar plot for shadow banks. For each shadow bank j, I find the number of banks that shadow bank j borrows from and have nonzero mortgage market overlap with j, divided by the total number of banks that shadow bank j borrows from. Black bars represent the distribution of Ratioj across shadow banks. Gray bars represent the benchmarks by plotting the distribution of Ratio′j⁠.

4.description: Upstream: Persistent warehouse lending relationships This figure shows the likelihood of having a warehouse lending relationship in quarters 1 to 25, conditional on having a warehouse lending relationship in quarter 0. The underlying sample is a panel data set that contains all possible pairs between warehouse banks and shadow banks. A warehouse lender is included in the data set if it conducts warehouse lending business and lends to at least one shadow bank in a particular quarter. To find the conditional probability, I find the first quarter of every warehouse lending relationship and define it as quarter 0 and then track these matched bank-shadow bank pairs for 25 quarters. The conditional probability in quarter t is calculated as the share of bank-shadow bank pairs that had a warehouse lending relationship in quarter 0 and still have a warehouse lending relationship after t quarters. As a benchmark, the dotted line plots the unconditional probability of having a lending relationship in each quarter. The unconditional probability in quarter t is calculated as the total share of bank-shadow bank pairs that have a warehouse lending relationship in quarter t, regardless of whether they had a warehouse lending relationship in quarter 0.

5.description: Transmission of bank net worth shock This figure plots the estimated coefficients of dynamic difference-in-differences regressions. Panel A corresponds to column 1 of Table 3. Panel B corresponds to column 3 of Table 3. Panel C corresponds to column 4 of Table 3. The vertical bars represent 90% confidence intervals. Standard errors are double clustered by shadow bank and bank.

6.description: The economic channels This figure shows the cannibalization channel (panels A–C) and the funding channel (panel D). In panels A–C, I compare the baseline model to a counterfactual world where vertically integrated (VI) banks do not price in the additional markup in their upstream decisions (Equation (7)). The magnitudes show the quantitative importance of the cannibalization channel for shadow banks’ funding costs in panel A, for mortgage rate charged by shadow banks in panel B, and for the share of shadow banks financed by vertically integrated banks in panel C. In panel D, I compare the baseline model to a counterfactual world where vertically integrated banks do not price in the additional markup in their downstream pricing decisions (Equation (9)). The magnitudes show the quantitative importance of the cannibalization channel for banks’ mortgage rate in panel D. In each panel, I show three scenarios: less concentrated markets (bottom decile), average markets, and more concentrated markets (top decile), which are defined based on the inverse of MSA-level number of mortgage lenders (i.e., 1/N).

7.description: Counterfactual: Warehouse duration This figure plots the impact of changing the warehouse duration η. Panel A shows changes in lending volume. To get total dollar volume, I set total market size F to be the average loan size ($354,500) times the population in need a mortgage (60% homeownership rate, 2 people per households, 10% of households looking for a house in a year, that is, 354,500× Population×60%×10%/2⁠). Panel B shows changes in consumer surplus. Consumer surplus is calculated as a lifetime present-value dollar equivalent measure of expected utility, which is integrated over consumer-specific preference shocks, while assuming a subjective discount rate of 4% over a period of 3 years. Panel C shows dollar flow through shadow banks via warehouse lending. Panel D shows intermediary profit.
